text: The patient is positioned laterally with the left side down and right side up. A 12mm operating port is placed on the left side, three finger-breadths superior or medial to the anterior superior iliac spine. Another fixed port is positioned in the midclavicular line just below the costal margin, typically a 5mm port. The camera is triangulated between these two ports.

The surgeon makes an incision in the posterior peritoneum just lateral to the reflection of the mesocolon using a harmonic scalpel. The incision is superficial, avoiding transgression of the Girota's fascia to facilitate colon retraction. The incision is made on the anterior surface of the lower pole of the kidney, allowing the liver to fall forward with gravity. The peritoneal layer is divided, and the lower pole of the kidney is retracted laterally, applying tension to separate the peritoneum from the fascia overlying Girota's fat. Minor adhesions are divided with the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon uses the dominant right hand for dissection while the left hand retracts the lower pole of the kidney laterally, creating space between the lower pole and the inferior vena cava (IVC). The incision of Girota's fascia exposes the duodenum, gonadal vein, and ureter. The ureter is identified lateral to the gonadal vein, reducing the risk of detaching the vein from the cava. The ureter is visible at the apex of the dissection triangle, allowing safe lifting with an instrument before division with clips.

The surgeon incises the Girota's fascia along the lateral wall, retracting the Girota's fat in continuity with the kidney to maintain an oncological barrier. The lateral peritoneal attachments of the kidney are divided, allowing the kidney to remain stable without falling medially. Effective retraction from the left hand aids in freeing the lateral margin of the kidney, facilitating access to the renal hilum.

The surgeon lifts the lower pole of the kidney superiorly and laterally, creating tension on the tissue within the triangle bordered by the lower margin of the renal vein and the lateral aspect of the inferior vena cava (IVC). The harmonic scalpel is used to stroke the edge of the right renal vein, freeing adherent tissue atraumatically. The surgeon attempts to locate the renal artery behind the right renal vein, but it is not immediately visible. To facilitate exposure, the anterior surface of the renal vein is revealed by incising the Girota's fascia in front of the vein, continuing the incision along the liver margin.

Above the superior edge of the renal vein, the renal artery is visible as a small knuckle of tissue protruding between two large branches of the renal vein. The renal vein has a common origin on the IVC with these branches. Further division of the Girota's fascia above the renal vein is performed to increase the mobility of the renal hilum, while avoiding transgression into the superior portion of the IVC.

The surgeon explores behind the renal vein to safely approach the renal artery, cleaning the fascia overlying the veins to allow mobilization. Using a right angle instrument, the surgeon divides adhesions between the vessels, taking care to avoid traumatic injury to the vein. With two renal veins present, one is divided, ensuring the other vein will continue to drain the kidney. Locking clips are applied and slid into position to secure the vein.

The surgeon applies the first clip easily, but the second clip fails to lock, possibly due to tissue caught in the clip or improper loading. Two clips are placed on the patient side of the vein, and one on the specimen side. The division of the vein provides access to the renal artery behind the remaining renal vein. The surgeon uses suction to stroke the fat overlying the artery, freeing adhesions atraumatically.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the lateral aspect of the renal artery, which is adherent to the residual renal vein, creating a space between the artery and vein. A linear stapler is applied, placing three rows of staples without cutting, followed by the placement of a locking clip medially and an additional clip on the renal side. The artery is then divided.

The surgeon tests for back filling of the renal vein by clamping it, observing no obvious filling. The renal artery is divided lateral to the staple line, and a small vasoarterium is cut just beyond the artery. Retraction is maintained while applying clips to control bleeding. Three locking clips are applied to the residual renal vein, which is divided between the lateral most clips. A single clip controls the small bleeding vasoarterium, allowing the surgeon to proceed with dissection of the upper pole.

The surgeon addresses bleeding by maintaining a clear view with retraction and camera positioning. Blood splashes on the camera are cleaned using suction irrigation to avoid interrupting the surgery. For small delicate vessels, clips are applied to control bleeding, while larger vessels like the renal vein side wall may require metallic clips arranged in a line or a non-cut linear stapler for more extensive bleeding control.

The surgeon performs the nephrectomy without the need for a fourth port, utilizing good retraction with the left hand. The camera port is strategically placed using a Hassan cut-down technique, considering the patient's body mass index, and positioned at a third point of a triangle formed by the iliac fossa port and the midclavicular subcostal port. The kidney is detached from the lateral abdominal wall, followed by inspection of the hilum and aspiration of any residual back bleeding.

The surgeon delivers the kidney through an extended iliac fossa incision using an endoscopic bag placed within the abdomen to scoop the kidney. The surgical wounds are then closed in a standard manner, completing the procedure.
